Claude.notes: Real-event story openers (ChatGPT launch, GPT-3 training, Attention Is All You Need, lawyers' fake citations) are accurate and well-chosen, but each is a single short paragraph that introduces a topic rather than dramatising the problem. The concept section then delivers the insight directly to the learner.
Claude.topIssue: Stories are too brief to make the learner land the insight themselves — the story sets the topic, then the concept section tells the answer.

Gemini.notes: Definitions of tokens, parameters, RLHF, transformers, attention, and hallucination are accurate at the appropriate depth. Two precision concerns: 'AI cannot truly originate' is presented as fact when it is a contested philosophical claim, and the $100M+ figure for GPT-4 training is a widely repeated estimate, not a confirmed figure.
Gemini.topIssue: The claim that AI 'cannot create something with zero precedent in its training data' is stated as a hard limit but is a contested interpolation/extrapolation question.

Perplexity.notes: Cited models stop at GPT-4 Turbo (128K) and Claude 3.5 (200K) from 2023–2024. By 2026 the frontier landscape has shifted considerably (Claude 4.x, larger context windows, new entrants), and the comparison table feels a generation behind.
Perplexity.topIssue: Context-window table tops out at Claude 3.5 / 200K — that snapshot is dated and undersells what current 2026 models offer.

Claude.notes: Word2Vec 'king − man + woman = queen,' the WGA strike, the Levidow lawyers, and the rise of 'prompt engineer' as a job title are well-paced narrative anchors. The prompt-craft lesson does a good job of turning the lab into a real exercise in writing better requests.
Claude.topIssue: Story openers are vivid but the protagonist (the learner) doesn't independently arrive at the insight — the concept section still does the heavy lifting after each vignette.

Gemini.notes: The Levidow lawyers / Judge Castel / six fictitious citations details are accurate. The Word2Vec analogy is correctly described. The $300K prompt-engineer salary claim is a 2023 anecdote at a small number of firms, not a typical figure, and would benefit from being labeled as an outlier.
Gemini.topIssue: Prompt-engineer salary figure ($300K) needs a disclaimer that it represents top-end outliers at a couple of firms, not a typical role.

Perplexity.notes: Most of the cited events (WGA 2023, lawyer sanctions 2023) remain canonical references. The 'prompt engineer' job market description has aged — by 2026 the role has largely been absorbed into product/applied-AI titles rather than persisting as a standalone $300K title.
Perplexity.topIssue: The 'prompt engineer at $300K' framing reflects 2023 hype; that job category has largely been re-bundled into AI product roles by 2026.

Claude.notes: Strongest narrative module so far. The Jason Allen / Colorado State Fair opener creates the exact 'is this art' tension the rest of the module unpacks, and Refik Anadol provides a counterweight to the Sarah Andersen lawsuit. Story-to-concept ratio is appropriate.
Claude.topIssue: Lab is conversational only — students debate prompt craft and ethics with the chat assistant but never actually generate or critique a real image.

Gemini.notes: The diffusion-model description ('starts with random noise and gradually refines it') is technically accurate at the level intended. 'Latent space' is described metaphorically as a map of concepts — defensible but loses the embedding-vector mechanism. Andersen et al. v. Stability AI is summarised correctly for a 2023 snapshot.
Gemini.topIssue: Latent-space treatment is metaphorical only; learners get no exposure to the idea of vector embeddings, which would be a small upgrade for this age.

Perplexity.notes: Anchor cases (Allen, Andersen, Trump fake-arrest images, Refik Anadol) remain the canonical ones for this topic. The lab does not name specific 2026-current image tools, so the practical 'do this now' angle is generic.
Perplexity.topIssue: Lab references to image-generation tools are generic — no mention of the 2025–2026 generation of tools (current Midjourney, Imagen, Flux, Sora image, etc.) that learners would actually open today.

Claude.notes: Daddy's Car (2016), MuseNet (2019), and 'Heart on My Sleeve' (2023) form a strong arc from 'AI can imitate' to 'AI can deceive at scale.' The ethics lab leans into the discomfort instead of resolving it — a strength.
Claude.topIssue: No actual music-making lab — students never generate or evaluate a clip from Suno/Udio, which is the obvious bridge between the lessons and 'applied' outcome.

Gemini.notes: Definitions of melody/harmony/rhythm and the explanation of why AI handles each differently are correct and well calibrated. The Musicians' Union UK 52% statistic is real (2023 survey). 'Fair use simplified' callout is accurate for US framing, though it could note that the US fair-use test does not apply outside the US.
Gemini.topIssue: The fair-use explainer is US-only but isn't labelled as such — international learners may take it as a global rule.

Perplexity.notes: 'Daddy's Car' (2016) and MuseNet (2019) are now historical curios; the live 2024–2026 stories — Suno v3/v4 and Udio launches, the RIAA suits filed in mid-2024, Universal/Anthropic settlement — are missing. 'Heart on My Sleeve' is still the strongest current case.
Perplexity.topIssue: Module skips the 2024 RIAA-vs-Suno/Udio lawsuits, which are now the defining current-events anchor for AI music ethics.

Claude.notes: Strongest module in the course. The Greg Rutkowski opener creates a personal stake the rest of the module pays off, and the labs (Fair Use Judge, Copyright Scorecard, Design the Rules) genuinely require the learner to apply reasoning rather than recite.
Claude.topIssue: Some narrative repetition with M3 on the Andersen v. Stability lawsuit, but the framing is sufficiently different here that it works.

Gemini.notes: The four-factor fair-use framework is reproduced accurately and applied correctly. The lawsuits are summarised faithfully for a 2023 snapshot; the module doesn't claim resolutions it doesn't have. Strong technical fidelity for a legally complex topic.
Gemini.topIssue: Lawsuit status is current as of late 2023 only — the partial summary judgments and dismissals from 2024 should be mentioned to keep the legal picture honest.

Perplexity.notes: Grounded in the durable cases (Rutkowski, Andersen, Getty, Authors Guild). Misses the 2024 NYT v. OpenAI suit and the EU AI Act (which took effect 2024–2026) — both are now central to any 2026 'rules of the road' conversation.
Perplexity.topIssue: EU AI Act and NYT v. OpenAI (filed Dec 2023, central by 2024) are the two biggest 'new rules' developments and should be in Lesson 4.

Claude.notes: Hollie Mengert / Hollie+ is a sharp opener — a specific named artist, a specific named model, a specific harm. The labs (Mapping Your Voice, Platform Audit, Personal AI Policy, Defense Plan) build on each other into a real takeaway artifact.
Claude.topIssue: Some overlap with M5 on training-data/consent narrative; the 'voice' framing differentiates it but the WGA strike anchor reappears verbatim.

Gemini.notes: LAION-5B at 5.85B image-text pairs is correct. Glaze is described accurately as a perturbation tool. 'Do Not Train' tags exist on ArtStation and DeviantArt as stated. One small precision issue: Glaze is described without mentioning Nightshade, its more aggressive sibling that learners would now encounter in the same conversation.
Gemini.topIssue: Glaze is named without mentioning Nightshade — the two are usually discussed together when learners look for current 'protect my work' tools.

Perplexity.notes: Practical advice (Glaze, opt-out tags, ToS audits) is still actionable in 2026. The lab outputs (audit, policy, defense plan) are evergreen because they're learner-specific. Missing: Cara, the artist-led platform that became the major 'no AI training' destination after Meta's 2024 ToS update.
Perplexity.topIssue: The platform-audit lab predates the 2024 Meta-ToS / Cara migration story — currently the most relatable 'where artists are moving' moment for learners.

modules.module: M7: AI as a Creative Partner

Claude.notes: Sunspring (2016), Théâtre D'Opéra Spatial, Heart on My Sleeve, and Zarya of the Dawn all reappear here from earlier modules — narrative material is reused rather than fresh. The Amplifier/Provocateur/Co-Author triad in Lesson 1 is the strongest original framing.
Claude.topIssue: Three of four story openers are recycled from earlier modules in this same course — narrative density suffers because returning learners have already seen these vignettes.

Gemini.notes: Definitions and case descriptions (Zarya, Heart on My Sleeve, Allen) are accurate but the technical depth doesn't add new substance over M3/M5. The four-element prompt framework (Subject/Style/Constraint/Perspective) is a clean schema.
Gemini.topIssue: Module reuses cases from M3 and M5 with similar framing — concept-accuracy passes, but it's hard to find new technical content for a learner who took the earlier modules.

Perplexity.notes: All cited cases are 2016–2023; nothing post-2023 anchors this module despite 'AI as creative partner' being one of the fastest-evolving practitioner conversations. The lab promises 'real AI tools you can use' but doesn't name any specific 2026-current ones.
Perplexity.topIssue: Lab claims to discuss 'real AI tools you can use' but lists none — by 2026 a learner expects specific named tools (Suno, Runway, Cursor, Sora, Claude, etc.) to anchor the conversation.

modules.module: M8: The Future of Creative Work

Claude.notes: WGA, Zarya, and Heart on My Sleeve return for a third or fourth time across the course — the narrative is competent but exhausted by Module 8. The Adobe / College Board partnership is a nice fresh anchor specifically for a 'future of work' framing for students.
Claude.topIssue: Module 8 leans on the same three or four cases the rest of the course already used; for the closing module of an 8-module course, learners deserve one or two anchors they haven't seen before.

Gemini.notes: The Goldman Sachs '300M jobs' figure is correctly attributed to their March 2023 report. The 'human authorship requirement' framing is correct US copyright doctrine. The 'new collar jobs' term is Ginni Rometty's coinage — could be cited to her, but the substance is right.
Gemini.topIssue: Goldman Sachs 300M-jobs estimate is from March 2023 — it should be labelled as a single early estimate, not the consensus, since later studies (OECD, McKinsey) have produced markedly different ranges.

Perplexity.notes: The 'if you are 14 today, you'll enter the workforce around 2030' framing was apt when written but is now uncomfortably close — the 'far-future' tone reads as 'next-year' tone in 2026. The labour-market data citations are pre-2024 and miss the actual 2024–2025 generative-AI hiring patterns.
Perplexity.topIssue: Future-tense framing ('what will happen by 2030') reads as imminent in 2026; the module should be reframed for a learner whose 2030 is now four years away, not ten.
